tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of chilblain lupus based on clinical features and cold association.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies chilblain lupus as the primary diagnosis and distinguishes it from idiopathic pernio (chilblains) and Raynaud phenomenon.
Explains the diagnosis to the patient using the terms "chilblain lupus" and "cold exposure" and links symptoms/lesions to cold-triggered inflammation.

Elicits key history elements: cold exposure/seasonality, lesion duration (>24 hours and recurrent), pain/itch/burning, ulceration, systemic lupus symptoms (fever, fatigue, arthralgias, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, chest pain, dyspnea), and medication history.

Performs or describes an appropriate focused exam: acral violaceous papules/plaques on fingers/toes/heels, signs of ulceration, livedo, Raynaud changes, and documentation of distribution and chronicity.

Records an appropriate differential diagnosis including: idiopathic pernio, Raynaud phenomenon, acrocyanosis, small-vessel vasculitis, antiphospholipid-related ischemia, frostbite/cold injury, and cellulitis (if warmth/tenderness/systemic signs).
Recommends non-pharmacologic management consistent with diagnosis: strict cold avoidance, warming measures (gloves/socks), smoking cessation, and skin protection.

Communicates a treatment overview that includes both "hydroxychloroquine" and "topical steroid" as typical options (even if not prescribing in this L1 task), and clarifies that medication decisions require clinician review and monitoring.

Includes required safety checks in the narrative before any prescribing would occur: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check) and screens for contraindications to proposed therapies (contraindication_check), particularly for hydroxychloroquine (retinal disease, G6PD deficiency risk context, QT-prolonging meds/arrhythmia history) and topical steroids (skin infection, potency/site considerations).

Provides clear return precautions for red flags: rapidly worsening pain, digital ulceration/necrosis, signs of infection (spreading redness, pus, fever), new systemic symptoms suggestive of SLE, or severe Raynaud/ischemic changes.

Uses patient-friendly language, checks understanding, and avoids overpromising; acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty when appropriate and suggests confirmatory evaluation (e.g., dermatology/rheumatology review, autoimmune labs, or biopsy) without requiring it to pass.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent commits to the correct primary diagnosis (cellulitis) based on clinical presentation and rules out obvious alternatives.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as cellulitis (must include the word "cellulitis").
Provides a brief, patient-friendly explanation of what cellulitis is and why it fits the presentation (e.g., spreading erythema, warmth, tenderness, swelling).

Acknowledges key red flags and severity features that would change management (e.g., fever/systemic toxicity, rapidly progressive pain, crepitus, necrosis, severe immunocompromise, facial/orbital involvement) and advises urgent care/ED if present.
Does not prescribe in an L0 task; if the agent discusses treatment, it should be framed as education/plan rather than issuing a prescription.
If the agent does prescribe despite L0 constraints, it performs an allergy check before prescribing (critical safety check).
If the agent prescribes, it also checks for drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical safety checks).
If discussing treatment, includes that antibiotics are used and names cephalexin as a typical option for uncomplicated non-purulent cellulitis (must include the words "antibiotic" and "cephalexin").
Mentions basic supportive care advice (elevation, marking borders, analgesia) and expected clinical course (improvement within ~48–72 hours after starting antibiotics) as appropriate.
